headlines.title: Police arrest 3 suspects in downtown San Jose shooting
headlines.description: Three suspects tied to a February shooting at a downtown San Jose business are in custody, police said Thursday. The shooting occurred about 12:45 a.m. on Feb. 15 inside a business in the first block of South Second Street. A man had been arguing with the suspects when one of them pulled out a gun and allegedly shot the victim multiple times, according to police. The victim was transported to a local hospital with non-life-threatening injuries. The suspected shooter, later identified as Sean James Jr., was restrained by employees before being taken into custody by police. A privately-made gun that allegedly belonged to James was found at the scene. The remaining suspects fled before police arrived. James was booked into the Santa Clara County Main Jail on suspicion of attempted murder and various firearm-related crimes. San Jose gang investigations detectives identified the three other suspects as Isaac Ortiz-Guerrero, Marteis Sims and Gabriel Martinez. On May 14, Sims was arrested in San Leandro, and Ortiz-Guerrero and Martinez were arrested in San Jose. While carrying out search warrants, detectives allegedly located gang indicia, as well as evidence of the shooting. In addition, an illegal firearm was allegedly found at Ortiz-Guerrero’s home. All three were booked into Santa Clara County Main Jail on suspicion of attempted murder. Anyone with information about the case or similar cases is asked to contact Detective Moreno #4882 and/or Detective Guevara #4669 of the San Jose Police Department Gang Investigations Unit via email: 4882@sanjoseca.gov and/or 4669@sanjoseca.gov, or (408) 277-3835.

headlines.title: Judge finds teenager responsible for murder in Santana Row fatal stabbing
headlines.description: A Santa Clara County juvenile justice court judge on Friday ruled that a then-13-year-old boy is responsible for second-degree murder following the deadly 2025 Valentine’s Day stabbing of another boy at Santana Row in San Jose. The ruling came after a monthslong juvenile court trial centered on the killing of 15-year-old David Gutierrez, who was attacked on Feb. 14, 2025. Following the ruling, Santa Clara County District Attorney Jeff Rosen said prosecutors will ask the judge to sentence the now-14-year-old defendant to seven years in a secure juvenile facility. Gutierrez’s family said they believe justice will only be served if the sentence amounts to years, not months. “We’re not gonna be scared to speak up,” Gutierrez’s aunt, Dianna, said. “We’re never gonna be scared to speak up for David. So, our family is really, really sad right now (sob) but at the same time we’re gonna celebrate this victory.” Defense attorneys argued during trial that the boy acted in self-defense because he believed Gutierrez had a knife. San Jose police said investigators found no evidence that Gutierrez was armed. It remains unclear how the court could impose the seven-year recommendation under California laws governing juvenile offenders who were 13 or younger at the time of a crime. “The minor was 13.5 at the time,” Rosen said after the ruling. Gutierrez’s mom also spoke outside court Friday, supporting the judge’s decision. “ It’s a tragedy for me and my family… but I don’t know if we will ever be able to recover from it,” Veronica Gutierrez said. “I feel like today the judge did the right thing. She was fair. It was very clear that David got attacked and killed for no reason.” Authorities said the then-13-year-old defendant and four other teenagers confronted Gutierrez because he was wearing red, which they associated with a rival gang. San Jose police said Gutierrez had no gang ties and was wearing red because it was Valentine’s Day. The judge found the boy responsible for the assault and the deadly stabbing that followed moments later. The judge also found the defendant guilty in a separate assault at Valley Fair mall in which prosecutors said he attacked another person and stole a gold necklace and a shoe. Two additional charges remain unresolved. The only adult charged in the case, Emmanuel Sanchez Damian, remains in custody and his case is pending. Two other teens who were 16 at the time of the attack were previously found responsible in Gutierrez’s assault case. One was sentenced to two years in juvenile hall. The now-14-year-old defendant is scheduled to return to court July 1. San Jose May 18 San Jose Mar 20 San Jose Aug 5, 2025 San Jose May 4, 2025 Group holds rally for teen killed in Santana Row stabbing

headlines.title: Caltrans begins final weekend closure of SF's Highway 1 (19th Avenue)
headlines.description: The third and final weekend closure of a busy stretch of San Francisco’s Highway 1 (19th Avenue) began Friday morning. Caltrans shut down Highway 1 (19th Avenue) in both directions between Sloat Boulevard and Holloway Avenue so crews can complete the final phase of a major repaving project. The full closure started at 7 a.m. Friday and is scheduled to last through 10 a.m. Monday. Transportation officials are urging drivers to avoid the area if possible and use alternate routes, including Sloat Boulevard and Junipero Serra Boulevard. Caltrans also asked pedestrians to stay on sidewalks and avoid walking in the street or on medians through the construction zone because of heavy equipment operating in the area. San Francisco May 11 San Francisco May 8 San Francisco Apr 23 Crews have already completed two previous weekend closures as part of the paving work, and newly repaved lanes are visible along parts of the corridor. Caltrans officials said completing the work over three weekend closures will allow crews to finish the project in about 10 days. Officials said the work would have taken about 40 days if lanes had remained partially open during weekday construction.

headlines.title: Ebola outbreak concerns grow as fans prepare to travel for the World Cup
headlines.description: The Ebola outbreak is raising global concerns, just as thousands of fans prepare to travel for the World Cup. The Santa Clara County Department of Public Health says it’s been preparing for months ahead of the World Cup, including how to monitor infectious diseases. While health experts say containing the outbreak is critical, it also says the risk of Ebola spreading in the U.S. remains low. Preparations are well underway for the FIFA World Cup across the United States. The tournament will bring hundreds of thousands of visitors from around the world, but as the countdown continues, so does the push to contain a deadly Ebola outbreak in Africa. The CDC reports nearly 600 suspected cases and 148 suspected deaths. That concern reached North America Wednesday, when an Air France flight to Detroit was diverted to Canada after a passenger from the Democratic Republic of Congo was allowed to board in error. The U.S. is now restricting entry for non-U.S. citizens who have been in Congo, South Sudan, or Uganda within the past three weeks. Americans returning from those regions will be routed through Dulles International Airport for enhanced screening. With the World Cup drawing closer, the DRC’s National Team has canceled its pre-tournament camp amid the outbreak. FIFA tells NBC News it’s working with U.S. health agencies and continuing to monitor the situation. The team is still set to play in Houston, with a possible match in Dallas. Meanwhile, The Bay Area is expecting thousands of international visitors for six matches in Santa Clara. The county Department of Public Health says it has a plan in place to monitor people traveling from areas impacted by the outbreak. Health experts also stress that while Ebola is deadly, it does not spread easily like COVID-19. “You have to be super sick and in the middle of an outbreak and around people who are dying and very sick with those bodily fluid exposures to get sick, that’s not gonna be people who are coming here to the World Cup, very sick people are not going to be able to get on a plane, so I’m not worried about Ebola. Am I worried about other things like measles, influenza, anything else that can spread when a lot of people are together? Yes,” said Dr. Monica Gandhi, infectious disease doctor at UCSF. Gandhi also said she believes the U.S. withdrawal from the World Health Organization and USAID cuts may have delayed awareness of the outbreak. The state department denies that claim.

headlines.title: 2 displaced after house fire in South San Jose
headlines.description: Two residents were displaced by a fire Thursday night at a home in South San Jose, fire officials said. The fire was reported about 8:50 p.m. at a two-story home in the 400 block of Tanfield Lane. Crews had the fire knocked down by 9:34 p.m., according to the San Jose Fire Department. The Red Cross was called to assist two people displaced by the fire. Information on what started the fire was not available.
headlines.source: Victoria Meza and Bay City News

headlines.title: For Memorial Day weekend, California gas prices are the highest since 2022
headlines.description: The Memorial Day weekend travel rush has begun, and this year, the holiday weekend comes at a time when consumers are facing rising gas prices. According to AAA, this week, California’s average gas price was $6.14 a gallon, which is the highest the state has seen for this time of year, with the exception of 2022. The average cost of gas in California is up $1.26 from the same time last year, AAA said. AAA notes that this price places California as the market with the highest gas prices in the nation. Doug Johnson, a spokesperson for AAA Northern California, said that with the Strait of Hormuz still closed and with two California refineries recently shuttered, relief is not in sight yet. Plus, he noted that with more people looking to take summer road trips, demand for gas will increase, which could further increase prices. Still, Johnson predicts that many Californians will still opt to drive over the holiday. “They’re still gonna go on their vacation, but they’re going to wanna cut corners where they can,” he said. AAA forecasts that 5,867,966 will travel in California over the holiday, and of those, 4,779,297 people will travel by car. In Foster City, several drivers told NBC Bay Area they are still planning to keep up their Memorial Day plans despite the gas prices. “We’re still gonna keep doing what we have to do, we’re big on spending time with our family, so we’re definitely going to take that trip,” said Jeev Kumar of Foster City, who said it now costs around $160 to fill up the tank of his truck. Laura Callagy of Foster City said she paid $82 to fill up her car on Thursday. But she and her family are still planning to drive up near Yosemite for the weekend. “The air fares are crazy, I mean everything is expensive, so we just are driving, but it’s still very costly- it’s just too expensive, the Bay Area’s too expensive,” Callagy said, adding that they plan to fill up gas closer to Yosemite, where the gas is cheaper. According to AAA, these are the average gas prices for different Northern California cities this week: San Francisco: $6.33 Oakland: $6.22 San Jose: $6.16 Sacramento: $6.10 Fresno: $6.09 Stockton: $6.01 You might be able to save money on gas this holiday weekend by avoiding driving furing peak traffic hours. AAA said that the worst times to travel this holiday weekend will be Thursday and Friday from 3 p.m. to 6 p.m., as well as Monday afternoon. The best times to travel will be morning hours and Sunday, AAA noted.

headlines.title: Firefighters battle grass fire in Contra Costa County
headlines.description: Crews are battling a grass fire in Contra Costa County, according to Cal Fire. Cal Fire said the fire was reported in the area of Nortonville Road and Black Diamond Way near Clayton. The incident, dubbed Norton Fire, started just before 5:30 p.m., according to Cal Fire’s website. Cal Fire reported the flames initially burned up to 10 acres, but later said the fire grew until it reached 20 acres. Just before 7 p.m. Cal Fire stated the fire burned 40 acres, and forward progress has been stopped, but containment has been difficult. Contra Costa County 22 hours ago wildfires May 20 San Jose May 20 The cause of the fire is unknown. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates.

headlines.title: San Francisco Symphony appoints its first female music director
headlines.description: After a two-year research, the San Francisco Symphony named Elim Chan as its new music director, and she is the first woman to hold that position in the symphony’s history. The 39-year-old became the first woman who has been named to lead the San Francisco Symphony in its 115-year history. Chan grew up in Hong Kong and then studied at Smith College in Massachusetts and the University of Michigan. She’s conducted all over the world and is among the new female Asian American conductor at a major symphony. She starts in September.

headlines.title: Veterans working as security officers honor colleagues killed on the job in San Francisco
headlines.description: On Thursday, there was an early Memorial Day ceremony in San Francisco, as military veterans currently working as security officers gathered to honor their colleagues killed on the job. It was a makeshift memorial for security officers killed on the job. One of them was Amin Abdula, the San Diego security guard who was killed in Monday’s mosque shooting while protecting children and staff. “I’m standing up for the guards that are not here, I’m standing up for ones that lost their lives. In fact, we just had a security officer lose their life not too long ago. His name was Amin Abdula. He gave his life to save the mosque that he was protecting,” said Charles Person, a security officer. Person is a security officer and a member of a union representing 50,000 private security officers across the country. His union organized Thursday’s event. People left flowers and heard from speakers. “We’re not playing, we’re not begging for anything, we’re demanding what we deserve,” said Latasha Reed, a security officer. They say they’re pushing for a fair contract, a raise and improved standards in the industry. Additionally, they also want lawmakers to pass SB1203. “SB1203, in a nutshell, is making sure that we get the proper training and tools that we need to do our job proficiently and effectively,” But others say there is more to consider with the bill. “We’ve got the most training of any state in the United States. We always want to give more training, but at the end of the day comes down to money. It comes down to how do we get the money to afford to pay the training and then when we do get the training to the guards, we have to compensate them for taking the training,” said David Chandler, President of CALSAGA.

headlines.title: Watch live: Community members address police response to fight at Fairfield High School
headlines.description: Students at Fairfield High School walked out of class Thursday to protest how police responded to a fight on campus. Viral video taken by students shows an officer hitting one student in the face multiple times. Now, Fairfield police have released their own video they say tells the full story. “The child was already down on the ground. He posed no threat. There was no reason for the excessive force,” said Nickie Williams, a parent at Fairfield High School. “I really think it’s unnecessary and super violent. You guys should protect us, but you guys are the ones beating on us,” student CJ Johnson said. Students marched out of class to express their outrage, many calling on the police department to fire the officer. But on Thursday, the Fairfield Police Department released its own videos. The first video shows the student wrestling with a school staff member trying to break up a fight, while another video shows a police officer trying to detain the same teenager. The teen doesn’t comply and runs back into the crowd of brawling kids. A third video shows the officer finally escorting the student to the office. He is seen resisting. Police say the teen hit the officer and was acting aggressively that’s the officer used a take down maneuver to get the boy on the ground. The fourth video shows the boy with his hands over his face despite commands to put his hands behind his back. Police say the female officer administered distraction strikes to the boys’ hands to get him to comply and that indeed prompted him to finally place his hands behind his back. After the dust settled yesterday, the officer responded to some questions from students as they recorded her.

headlines.title: Here's why California's $456M 911 upgrade went sideways: sources, records indicate
headlines.description: Newly released internal records show insiders with California’s troubled $456 million Next Generation 911 project lost confidence in the system’s chief architect years before he left the state agency overseeing the overhaul. The project is intended to replace California’s aging emergency call system with internet-based technology capable of handling text messages, video, and more precise caller location data. The records, obtained under the California Public Records Act more than a year after the original request was filed, also shed light on what contributed to the departure of Budge Currier, the former Assistant Director of Public Safety Communication at the California Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES). In one 2024 email to a top Cal OES official, a regional Next Gen 911 coordinator wrote that Currier prioritized innovation over public safety. She doesn’t name him in the email but said later that she was referring to the now-retired California Next Gen 911 architect. “The project lacked clear direction, operational knowledge, and the one person really in control was willing to risk lives for the sake of innovation,” the regional coordinator wrote. She said she learned about these issues shortly after taking her role in January 2020. “The project lacked clear direction, operational knowledge, and the one person really in control was willing to risk lives for the sake of Innovation. When we clearly had proof of recklessness or engineering flaws, it was ignored or downplayed.” NG9-1-1 Regional Coordinator in 2024 NBC Bay Area previously documented cases in which dispatchers said routing failures and transfer problems delayed emergency response during real 911 calls. WATCH NOW: FULL NBC BAY AREA INVESTIGATION ON CA’S NEXT GEN 911 UPGRADE After years of delays and technical problems, a new leadership team is now overseeing the project. State officials say they are taking the system in a new direction with the goal of getting Southern California dispatch centers onto the upgraded network for the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics. “Through personnel changes, increased oversight, and contractor accountability, the state is now working toward a renewed vision for NG 9-1-1,” Cal OES said in a statement. Cal OES officials, however, even when pressed by state lawmakers during recent public hearings, have been reluctant to assign blame for past failures. Records and interviews, however, suggest broader concerns about oversight inside the agency during the project’s rollout. Internal Cal OES email obtained by NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit. But multiple project insiders who directly worked on the upgrade said Currier knew the technology had significant issues but approved it for deployment anyway. The sources mostly spoke anonymously because they still work in the industry and fear retaliation. “He had professional network engineers that would tell him, ‘This is not going to work, we need to talk about this, we need to change this,’” said the insider, who worked on the state’s Next Gen 911 team. “And he told them that, ‘We’re not going to change this.’” Separately, two high-level Cal OES sources told NBC Bay Area that Currier rejected attempts to hold vendors accountable and dismissed data they said identified critical flaws with the 911 network. The officials said Currier dismissed attempts to send out two dozen contract violation letters to vendors over problematic technology. “[Next Gen 911 officials] feared upsetting Budge,” that same project insider said. “I heard [Currier] threaten them once or twice while he was there that he could fire all of them if they didn’t do what he said.” “I heard [Currier] threaten them once or twice while he was there that he could fire all of them if they didn’t do what he said.” CA NG911 source Currier did not respond directly to questions about how he treated employees or allegations that he dismissed warning signs that pointed to problems with the technology. In a text message to NBC Bay Area, however, he said the processes to address any employee grievances or contract violation letters were enforced. In addition to highlighting early internal concerns about the project, the new records also help answer the question of why Currier left Cal OES long before the project was even close to being completed. Text messages to and from a Cal OES official and a 911 dispatch manager show the decision to leave may not have been his choice, even though Currier publicly said he retired. Shortly after parting ways with Cal OES in 2024, the dispatch manager, who was closely working with the state on the NG 911 transition, sent a text message to the number two Cal OES official on the project at the time, saying: “Well? Is he gone? Access removed? Spill the tea!” The Cal OES official responded, “He upset executive[s] a few too many times and got ahead of being fired by retirement.” Currier isn’t the only Cal OES leader who’s left the agency. The agency’s director Nancy Ward retired at the end of 2025 and deputy director Lisa Mangat, as of January 2026, became a retired annuitant for Cal OES. NBC Bay Area reached out to Cal OES requesting comment from Mangat and Ward. We did not hear back from the officials, but the agency said Ward “has dedicated her extraordinary career, spanning more than three decades, to protecting Californians and Americans before, during, and after disasters.” Regarding Mangat, Cal OES said in a statement, “Mangat has been a key leader supporting Californians in variety of capacities across multiple decades. Her service has made our state stronger.” While the records shed new light on past problems, newly appointed state officials say they’re now focused on finally bringing a modern 9-1-1 system to California. The agency said it’s committed to transparency around the project and posted a series of documents on its website outlining project timelines and past system failures. “We’re moving forward – deliberately and transparently – to modernize our emergency response with a statewide Next Generation 9-1-1 system,” an agency spokesperson said. Even as Cal OES promises a “renewed vision” for the project, lawmakers continue questioning whether the agency that oversaw years of delays should remain in charge of completing California’s new emergency response system before the 2028 Olympics. If you have a question or comment about this story, contact candice.nguyen@nbcuni.com.
headlines.source: Candice Nguyen, Michael Bott and Jeremy Carroll

headlines.title: 12-year-old accused of illegaly riding e-motorcycle in Danville
headlines.description: Police in Danville cited a 12-year-old who was allegedly speeding through a neighborhood on an electric motorcycle. Danville PD says an officer spotted the kid running a stop sign and speeding through the neighborhood at 45 miles per hour. The officer followed him for a mile before discovering the rider was 12 years old. Police say the motorcycle was capable of speeds up to 60 miles per hour and required a driver’s license to operate. The officer cited the kid and his parents and towed the motorcycle. Police are urging parents to learn the difference between legal e-bikes, and e-motorcycles before buying one for your kid.
